data.title: IQMM: The $22 Billion Money Market ETF You Did Not Know About
data.description:
Thesis

When investors think about money market funds, they usually picture names priced at $1 per share that stay at that level over time. It used to be that way, and any 'breaking of the buck' would represent significant financial news. With the advent of retail-oriented trading platforms and exchange-traded funds, an entire suite of new products was derived that replaced traditional money market funds. And we are talking about T-Bill ETFs here, which came in full force.

However, money market funds have evolved, and they have managed to insert themselves into the ETF wrapper. We have reviewed one in the past, namely the iShares Prime Money Market ETF (PMMF). Today we are going to review a new entrant in the space that has amassed an astounding AUM exceeding $20 billion, and one that many investors have never heard of. And we are talking about the ProShares GENIUS Money Market ETF (IQMM) here.

What makes IQMM special?

Unlike traditional money market funds, IQMM is structured as an ETF; thus, it can be bought and sold throughout the day. But that is not the main selling point, especially when considering that iShares already came out with a competing product last year. What makes IQMM special is its compliance with the GENIUS Act:

The GENIUS Act (Guaranteeing Essential National Infrastructure in US Stablecoins) is the United States' first comprehensive legislation on stablecoins. This bill focuses on stablecoins, setting up regulatory guidelines for this form of digital currency across the United States. Stablecoin issuers must hold 1:1 reserves for any stablecoins issued. These reserves can be held in physical currency, US treasury bills, repurchase agreements and other low-risk assets approved by regulators. Issuers are required to report reserve composition and are subject to regular audits by registered public accounting firms.

Without getting too much into the crypto world structure and nomenclature, an investor needs to only understand that IQMM is now a regulatory compliant fund to hold required cash from the crypto universe. The ability to have a regulatory compliant ETF moves the crypto universe away from potential bank bankruptcies or other systemic financial system issues. It is not a surprise the fund's AUM is an astounding $22 billion now:

For those investors not very familiar with the crypto universe, suffice it to mention that one of the stablecoin providers had cash with Silicon Valley Bank (SVB), which went bankrupt:

In the early Saturday morning hours, the Silicon Valley Bank (SVB) failure caused USDC to de-peg from $1. The price dipped to almost $0.88 before recovering to around $0.96 at the time of writing. The reason for the de-peg is that Circle, the company issuing USDC, has $3.3 billion in reserves tied up at SVB. This is money backing the value of USDC, and if it was to be lost as part of SVB's failure, it would mean a financial hit for Circle.

The ability to achieve regulatory compliance via an ETF should not be underestimated, and we anticipate other providers of ETFs to jump in this arena shortly. The net expense ratio is 0.15%, which is in line with what other ETFs holding bills charge:

    • F/m US Treasury 3 Month Bill Fund ETF (TBIL) expense ratio: 0.15%
    • iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV) expense ratio: 0.09%

    • State Street SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL) expense ratio: 0.13%

There is a fee waiver through January 2027, though, with the expense ratio going to 20 bps after.

Weekly payments and other analytics

The fund is very investor friendly, set-up with weekly payments and competitive yields:

  • 30-day SEC yield: 3.52%
  • Payment frequency: weekly
  • NAV strike: $100/share
  • Weighted average portfolio maturity: 1 month
  • Inception date: February 2026
  • AUM: $21.8 billion

Most T-Bill ETFs have monthly distributions and NAV levels that vary. We like the visual aspect for IQMM, which has a $100/share target NAV, thus reminding older investors of the 'buck' for money market funds structured in the mutual fund format.

No credit or duration risk

The ETF only holds T-Bills, thus has no credit risk. The fund is also structured with a very low duration, with the average portfolio maturity at 34 days. This translates into no duration risk either.

The fund is an elegant solution to a regulatory requirement, but it was structured smartly to have an easy-to-understand NAV, frequent distributions, and ultra-safe composition.

Given its size, the ETF comes with significant liquidity, which is set to move even higher. We anticipate much higher AUMs here until other fund managers come to the market with competing products.

While crypto universe treasurers are probably jumping with joy given the creation of IQMM, retail investors are also set to benefit from the massive liquidity provided by this ETF as well as its frequent weekly payments. The name comes with a competitive yield and fees, and thus is an appealing choice for parking cash.

Dollar parking for crypto

One of the initiatives of the current president resides with integrating the crypto universe within the financial system so that demand is created for dollars and treasuries. While we are not experts in crypto, having stablecoins pegged and required to hold dollar-based treasuries ensures a bid for dollars and the U.S.-centric approach to the global financial system. We have seen renewed calls from many BRICS countries for transactions to occur in Chinese yuan in order to move away from the regulatory framework imposed by the dollar, but little success in debunking the U.S. currency.

The GENIUS Act and funds like IQMM cement the importance of U.S. T-Bills and ensure the crypto framework is very well linked to the U.S. money market universe.

Conclusion

IQMM is a new money market ETF from ProShares. The fund came to market in February 2026 but already has assets in excess of $20 billion. The fund is compliant with the GENIUS Act and offers weekly interest payments. The ETF holds only T-bills with a very low duration, thus having virtually no credit or interest rate risk. The fund is structured with a visually appealing $100/share NAV, thus making it an appealing place to park cash.

data.title: GPIQ: Goldman Sachs Built The Income ETF I Wish Existed 5 Years Ago
data.description:
I have been investing in and writing about income-producing assets for a long time now. I have an entire segment of the portfolio structured around generating cash flow from equities. I spend quite a bit of time tracking and analyzing my income producing assets and I believe that the current market environment is an attractive entry point for long-term investors into income producing assets. The big thing about covered call strategy ETFs that people trend to glance over is that they are not conservative vehicles for people afraid of stocks just because they have a large distribution yield. They are structured to pay out a large amount of income for capping some of your upside during periods when nobody knows what is coming next. I believe that the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) is built for this exact moment. The Iranian conflict has caused the flow of roughly 20% of global oil supply to become constricted as the Strait of Hormuz has become the most important global choke point. We're headed into the heart of Q1 2026 earnings with big banks setting the stage for big tech later in the month. The market is going to demand proof that Big Tech AI spending is translating into real revenue causing uncertainty which could translate to increased options premiums. GPIQ is paying a distribution of $5.33 which is a yield of roughly 10.42% at a time when GPIQ is only down -2.7% for the year. I think that GPIQ will continue to generate low double digit or high single digit yields while returning positive appreciation throughout the year.

Following up on my previous article about GPIQ

Back in November I had written an article on GPIQ (can be read here) and since then the total return is 2.82% due to the large distribution compared to the S"&P 500 gaining 1.69%. I felt that GPIQ offered a compelling blend of capital appreciation and double-digit yield as the dynamic covered call strategy left the upside partially uncapped. This allowed GPIQ to benefit from market appreciation while producing a large recurring income stream for investors. Despite the evolving Fed policy GPIQ's distribution rate has been stable and I felt it had become more attractive in a failing rate environment. I am following up with a new article on GPIQ because I feel the current environment is creating opportunities for covered call ETFs especially with how uncertain the geopolitical landscape has been. In my opinion, GPIQ is built for periods like this as it can generate large amounts of recurring income when the market falls and follow the market higher on green days since a portion of the portfolio is uncapped.

What GPIQ Actually Is And How It Works

GPIQ launched in October 2023, so it does not have as long of a track record as some of the other covered call ETFs such as the Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD). While this isn't a long enough timeframe for some investors what GPIQ does have is a clear and well executed strategy backed by Goldman Sachs Asset Management, and results that speak for themselves. GPIQ has constructed an equity portfolio that mirrors the Nasdaq-100 index. The top holdings look exactly like what you would expect with Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) representing 8.74% of the portfolio and Apple (AAPL) coming in at 7.67%. The tech sector makes up roughly 51% of the portfolio, with communication services at about 15% and 104 positions.

Where GPIQ gets interesting is the overlay segment of its strategy. It sells call options on anywhere between 25% and 75% of the underlying holdings which is the key differentiator. This is what Goldman calls a dynamic overwrite strategy which is significantly different than covered call ETFs operating at fixed percentages. QYLD writes calls on 100% of its portfolio every month while the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) utilizes equity-linked notes which have their own structural limitations. GPIQ can actively adjust how much of the portfolio is being overwritten based on market conditions which is what I like about it.

When volatility spikes and premiums expand, GPIQ has the ability to overwrite a larger portion of the portfolio. This allows them to collect larger premiums and deliver more income to shareholders. When the market trends higher GPIQ has the ability to reduce the overwrite percentage and let more of the underlying equity appreciation drive the share price higher. That flexibility is why GPIQ has managed to deliver both a double-digit yield and better total returns than most of its peers. GPIQ also utilizes FLEX options through the CBOE which are customizable exchange-traded option contracts. This gives the portfolio managers the ability to tailor strike prices and expiration dates in ways that standard listed options cannot match.

The taxed advantaged income profile is a benefit for income investors

GPIQ has paid $5.33 in distributions over the trailing twelve months (TTM) which is a 10.42% yield. The part that doesn't get enough attention is the tax treatment of these distributions. A large portion of the distributions get treated as return of capital because GPIQ uses index options that qualify for Section 1256 tax treatment. The gains are split on a 60/40 basis between long-term and short-term regardless of holding period. When I considered this against ETFs that are taxed as ordinary income GPIQ looks increasingly favorable especially at the end of the year when Uncle Same gets is fair share. For some investors this aspect can become extremely favorable especially when you have larger amounts of income being generated from the underlying investment.

Since the end of 2023 GPIQ has paid 29 monthly distributions which have amounted to $12.26 of income. GPIQ opened at $38.78 and since 2023 has appreciated by 32.70% while producing another 31.71% in monthly distributions. Since it went public GPIQ has generated a total return of 64.32%. When I look at the characteristics of GPIQ the dynamic income approach looks very appealing as the monthly distribution has averaged $0.42 over the past 29 months which is a monthly yield of 0.82% based on the current share price. From an income perspective this works in my portfolio and I am very bullish on GPIQ going forward because its structure will benefit when the geopolitical tensions finally get sorted out while maintaining an attractive yield compared to the risk free rate of return.

Why Earnings Season Makes The GPIQ idea Timely

Q1 2026 earnings season is about to get started as JPMorgan Chase (JPQ) will set the tone on Tuesday 4/14. Big tech will come right as we have the April FOMC meeting at the end of the month. The consensus expectations for the S&P 500 are about 13.2% YoY earnings growth for Q1. Technology has seen its YoY earnings growth rate increase to 45.1% from 34.3% on December 31st. The market is looking for another strong quarter from the names that dominate the Nasdaq-100 which would be beneficial for GPIQ. The March washout shook out a lot of the speculative froth causing a repricing of mega-cap tech. It looks like there has been a lot of AI fatigue and the market wants proof that the amount of capital being spent on CapEx is going to pay off. I think were setting up for a scenario where the companies that deliver strong results are going to get rewarded and we're going back to good news is good news and bad news is bad news. This kind of dispersion would be beneficial for GPIQ's covered calls strategy because it keeps implied volatility elevated across the index.

If the Magnificent 7 come in and report strong Q1 numbers, GPIQ would participate meaningfully in the upside because it can dial back its overwrite percentage. If earnings come in mixed and the market chops sideways or pulls back further it still has the ability to generate that 10% plus yield that everyone loves collecting. This is a setup where the fund gets paid either way and the only question is how much capital appreciation comes along with it. The reality is that nobody except the management teams know what these companies are going to produce and more importantly what they will guide for. What we do know is that the implied volatility embedded in Nasdaq-100 options right now is elevated relative to where it was for most of 2024 and early 2025. This has become a direct input as to how much income GPIQ can generate. When the VIX was sitting at 13 or 14, covered call funds had to work harder for their yield and now with the Vix over 20 the premiums are significantly larger.

GPIQ is outperforming it's peers since inception and it's not even close

I compared GPIQ to the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), the SPDR S&P 500 Trust (SPY), QYLD, JEPQ, and the Neos Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI) since it's first day of trading and the results were overwhelmingly bullish. The metrics that I track are the starting price, todays price, how much appreciation was generated, the amount of income produced, and what the total return was. All of the data is in the table below. I am not as shocked as some may be to learn that GPIQ has almost doubled the total return of QYLD and been able to maintain a total annualized return that has almost matched the market.

Since inception GPIQ has generated $12.68 of capital appreciation while producing $12.68 in distribution income. This has led to a total return of 64.32% from a combination of 32.70% appreciation and 31.62% in distribution yield. The annualized return on GPIQ has been 22.37%. SPY which is the benchmark has a total return of 64.76% over this period with a 22.50% annualized return. GPIQ's performance is within a half of percent of SPY. QQQ which is the benchmark for the Nasdaq 100 has a total return of 71.97% with an annualized return of 24.65%. When I look at JEPQ, QQQI, and QYLD there is a huge drop off in total return since GPIQ hit the market. JEPQ has a total return of 53.18% while QQQI and QYLD both have the same total return at 36.30%. The data is clear and GPIQ is holding its own against the market and currently superior to its peers.

The risks to investing in GPIQ

Even though I am bullish on GPIQ there are several risks to consider. The first risk is concentration considering over 50% of this fund is in technology. If the Tech sector doesn't perform this earnings season we could experience a multi-quarter bear market in tech which will impact GPIQ's share price. The premium income provides a cushion but it's not going to save investors from a large step down in Nasdaq 100. . During the April 2025 tariff shock, GPIQ fell roughly 25% peak to trough before recovering. Next, if we get a ripping bull market due to the geopolitical tensions easing then GPIQ will underperform QQQ as the The covered call overlay inherently caps some upside. That is the trade-off as investors are trading some capital appreciation potential for current income. If your goal is pure growth maximization GPIQ isn't the right investment for you. The reality is that GPIQ has only been in existence for 2.5 years and we have not seen it navigate a prolonged recession or a sustained bear market. The dynamic overwrite strategy looks great in a volatile but ultimately recovering market but there is no data as to how its overwrite strategy will perform during a genuine downturn. Investors should do their own due diligence and make sure this strategy is right for them.

Conclusion

One of the things I think about is if I was going to build building an income-focused portfolio from scratch today would I want a vehicle that gives me exposure to the best companies while paying a double-digit yield that is tax-advantaged? The answer is yes and GPIQ checks off all the boxes especially in this market environment. We are in a market where volatility is elevated and earnings season is about to inject a fresh round of uncertainty. The Fed is not likely to cut rates and the geopolitical landscape is a mess. All of those factors push implied volatility higher which pushes option premiums higher and positively impacts GPIQ's distribution income. GPIQ doesn't need optimal conditions or a rising market to work and it's built for the types of uncertain environments we're living through. I am still bullish on GPIQ and adding to my position.

data.title: CBL & Associates Properties: Dividend Hike Offsets AFFO Guidance Disappointment
data.description:
Introduction

Shares in CBL & Associates Properties (CBL) have recovered from early 2026 weakness and are now notably higher year-to-date, continuing their streak of outperformance relative to U.S. REIT peers. This comes as the company reported solid 2025 results but issued weak 2026 guidance.

I think that some of the drivers behind the 2026 AFFO decline (such as elevated share based compensation and higher fixed-rate funding costs) should prove to be short-lived, confirming my previous Buy rating on the shares. The bullish investment thesis behind CBL can be summarized as:

  • CBL offers a 12.7% market-implied cap rate, quite attractive even if net operating income continues to stagnate.
  • CBL trades at only 6.2x its expected AFFO in 2026, with AFFO likely to return to growth in 2027-2028 thanks to lower funding costs and ongoing (albeit smaller) debt principal paydowns.
  • CBL recently hiked its annual dividend to $2.50/share, potentially reflecting increased confidence in operating performance and the company's current leverage profile.

2025 Results Recap

CBL reported AFFO of $7.21/share in 2025, a 7.8% Y/Y increase relative to 2024. Results benefited from a 0.5% increase in same-center net operating income [NOI], as well as portfolio growth from recent acquisitions. This was only partially offset by a 0.3% decline in occupancy to 90%.

Tenant sales increased 2.8%, largely in line with inflation, supporting a 2.6% increase in rent levels on new/renewal leases.

All in all, I think CBL's operating performance continues to reflect a challenging leasing environment as evidenced by the decline in occupancy. The silver lining is that occupancy should stabilize as tenant sales appear firmly in positive territory, notwithstanding downside risks from weaker-than-expected U.S. economic momentum.

Refinancing Drives Dividend Growth

Post year-end, CBL has been able to refinance roughly 25% of its outstanding debt, most notably achieving lower required debt amortizations. In essence, lenders will require CBL to pay down less debt principal each year.

This in turn increased the amount CBL can distribute as dividends, with the company expecting to be able to maintain a dividend level of $2.5/share in 2026, a 39% increase compared with the previous regular dividend. Relative to the current share price of about $42.68/share, this represents a dividend yield of about 5.86% achieved with a modest 0.34x 2025 AFFO payout.

To sum up, while lower debt amortizations will weigh on AFFO growth as I will discuss in the next section, I see the increased dividend level as a sign of confidence in CBL's ability to maintain current operating performance. What is more, with a rising share price, the relative necessity to pay down debt principal decreases, as net debt currently accounts for only 63% of the $3.5 billion enterprise value, down from 76% when I first covered the company in 2024.

Weak 2026 AFFO Outlook

In contrast to the increased dividend level, CBL actually expects AFFO to decline in 2026, reaching a level of $6.74-7.06/share, or down roughly 4% at the $6.90 midpoint. This is notably below my initial expectations for an AFFO of above $7.70/share. With same-center NOI seen roughly flat Y/Y, I see several driving factors behind the weak guidance:

  • Higher share count of $31.2 million, or a circa 1% drag on AFFO. In hindsight, CBL did not take advantage of a cheap share price to fully execute its $25 million share buyback. At the same time, stock-based compensation is increasing thanks to robust CBL share price performance.
  • Higher fixed-rate debt costs. While CBL has benefited from Fed rate cuts on its floating interest rate debt exposure (-0.77% Y/Y to 6.89% in 2025), interest costs on fixed-rate debt have increased by 0.33% to 5.51%, with a recent refinancing at 9.1% (admittedly for a non-recourse loan on a property that had cashflow restrictions in place) reflecting continued refinancing headwinds on fixed-rate debt. I should note that the drag is notably smaller if we look at other recent transactions, such as the 6.46% fxed rate loan used to purchase the Gateway Mall in Lincoln, Nebraska.
  • Lower debt principal paydowns. With a higher dividend level in place, CBL has less dry powder to pay down debt principal, which will naturally weigh on AFFO growth.

Coupled with Fed funds futures pricing for no Fed rate cuts this year, it appears that AFFO growth should only tick up in H2 2027 and into 2028, when the full effect of anticipated Fed policy normalization to 3% will become fully visible in CBL financials.

Valuation and Rating

CBL generated $447 million in 2025 net operating income, reflecting a market-implied cap rate of about 12.7% relative to the $3.5 billion enterprise value. While same-center NOI growth of 0.5% in 2025 and a roughly flat reading expected for 2026 are arguably uninspiring, CBL's high cap rate more than compensates for the lack of NOI growth. As such, as long as tenant sales increase in line with inflation and occupancy stabilizes around current levels, CBL appears to be in a good place from an enterprise-level perspective.

Relative to the 2026 AFFO midpoint of $6.90/share, the AFFO multiple stands at about 6.2x, which is quite cheap for a commercial REIT. Of course this reflects the still high net debt proportion in enterprise value of 63%, which naturally results in a low AFFO multiple.

What is more, I do see some of the AFFO growth headwinds as temporary. For instance, while investors naturally hope for a higher share price, a moderation in CBL stock returns (which will undoubtedly come at some point) will reduce the drag from share based compensation. Likewise I expect the Fed to resume rate cuts in 2027, allowing for higher AFFO thanks to lower funding costs in 2028.

All in all, while a strong performance year-to-date and limited near-term catalysts arguably warrant a more cautious outlook in the next few months, I continue to believe that CBL remains attractively valued on both an enterprise-level and AFFO-multiple basis, confirming a Buy rating on the company. Indeed at some point NOI should start to grow in line with tenant sales, while the resumption of Fed rate cuts will provide another boost to AFFO in late 2027 and into 2028.

Risks

CBL takes great pride in its non-recourse debt structure. Coupled with a higher market capitalization, the absolute amount of debt is arguably less of a concern in 2026. That said, I do expect CBL to continue to allocate roughly $50-60 million per year for debt principal paydowns, allowing for a gradual reduction in indebtedness over the next decade. Under baseline economic assumptions, the company is in good shape and should see no problems with reducing its still sizable debt pile.

During recessions and periods of consumer spending uncertainty however, scrutiny over CBL's indebtedness is likely to increase. In this respect, the recent increase in U.S. recession probabilities to 30% by Goldman Sachs (GS) acts as a warning sign for CBL investors that downside risks have unfortunately increased.

High leverage aside, a deterioration in operating performance is the other principal risk in the CBL investment case. I think CBL's valuation is only attractive as long as we see flat NOI growth in perpetuity (the good news is that no growth is currently priced in, hence the flat 2026 outlook is no real disappointment). That said, lower occupancy and increased tenant bankruptcies may actually push NOI growth into negative territory, which would dash any hopes for a resumption of AFFO growth next year.

Conclusion

CBL stock has recovered from an early 2026 slump and is outperforming U.S. REIT peers held in the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ). I believe this is largely driven by a sizable dividend increase even as the company issued a lackluster AFFO guidance for 2026.

The silver lining is that AFFO should return to growth in 2027, driven by a resumption in Fed rate cuts and continued (albeit smaller than expected) debt principal paydowns. This makes CBL a compelling investment for investors with a long-term horizon, notwithstanding near term risks from a U.S. recession which may weigh on operating performance and increase scrutinity of the company's still sizable debt pile.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: American Resources: Accelerated Production Buildout Has Me Convinced (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I’m not one to shy away from writing about companies where the price moves haven’t been that great. Today I’m tackling one of the companies I covered at the beginning of this year, American Resources (AREC). The stock price is down some 30% since my coverage. I did have a neutral stance, so there is that at least. Outright buying seemed like a hasty decision back then, but now I think the case is different.

Since the start of 2026 quite a lot of news has come out and it seems the market hasn’t been reacting all that well given how the price has moved. From Seeking Alpha it seems that the next report we’ll get is in the middle of May, which means quite a long wait since the last one back in November. Part of that is because of delays, but what is perhaps weighing on the stock right now I think will quickly fade as AREC scales its production this year. My model shows that the stock is trading at a very conservative level. With feedstock secured as well the risk profile got a lot better and I’m confident in raising my initial rating of a Hold to a Buy now. If conditions continue to improve this year, I’m not going to exclude that a Strong Buy might come eventually.

What’s Happening Under The Hood?

One of the major updates that has come out is how ReElement has now expanded production to four lines with a capacity of 16,000 metric tonnes. First production is expected to happen in Q3 of this year.

The growth story of AREC is now made up of two segments, Electrified Materials and ReElements Technologies. The latter being the spinoff of which AREC now holds a 19% minority stake. This comes as in Q1 of 2025 AREC officially shifted the business model:

That quote is from my last article and I think encapsulated exactly what the thesis is right now for AREC and how it has changed over the past few years as it changed direction. The main reason why this news is so considerable and for whatever reason hasn’t been reflected in the share price is that my initial revenue projection just got quadrupled.

For the initial phase, Phase 1, we're looking at 4,000 metric tons/year. Using the same price per kilo as before and only looking at Lithium, it implies about $33.88 million in revenues. Is this reasonable to expect for 2027?

In my model I was looking at 4,000 MT in Phase 1, AREC is now targeting 16,000 instead as mentioned earlier. On top of this the management can be quoted saying that the buildout is both on time and under budget. This is a shift from what has perhaps haunted the company in the past, namely project delays and other forms too. On March 31 AREC filed that the 10-K for 2025 would be coming out later, filing a 12b-25 form. The reason given seems to have been "unreasonable effort and expense” as for the delay. It’s no wonder that we see the stock price move downwards with news like this, it trumps any positive ones like the great capacity increase AREC is doing this year.

it'll be probably the middle of the later 2025 as we get Marion fully producing

But I also mentioned other delays, not just filings. The most glaring being for the Marion facility which was supposed to be operational last year, based on a 2024 call with management.

So the landscape is a little like this now, AREC has a poor history of delays, and some of the recent news further support that statement. But, it’s also now supposedly set on having a 16,000 metric tonne capacity by Q3 this year. In my January article 4,000 metric tonnes got me to $33.88 million in revenues, with 4x more by Q3 it jumps to $135.5 million instead, if AREC realizes 100% of the revenues, which of course it won't. Because of the 19% minority stake it looks to be closer to $25.7 million instead. Still, for a company with a market cap of just $237 million right now it makes for a very interesting case here, and ultimately why I’m confident upgrading my rating.

The Mitsubishi Collaboration

Before I go any further into how AREC can scale the Marion facility further, there was a recent announcement that AREC will be working with Mitsubishi Materials Corporation (MMC). The deal is going to help ensure that AREC has feedstock for ReElement such as end-of-life batteries and magnets.

Feedstock is perhaps the single most important topic for a company like AREC which relies on this to even generate any revenues. With a stable source of feedstock from MMC, the global materials conglomerate the operational risks for AREC I think are considerably lower now.

What Is The Top Line Going To Look Like This Year?

With the news of Phase 1 including 16,000 metric tonnes in capacity it means the revenue modeling I made last time needs a fresh update. The end product for ReElement is not lithium as a raw metal, it’s rather battery-grade lithium carbonate along with lithium hydroxide. Lithium Carbonate Equivalent is the most common price chart you’ll find and it refers to this exact product. Since December 2025 the price has rallied from $15 per kilo to around $24 right now and stabilized.

Those who are quick with their math might see that my earlier revenue mention of $135 million needs to be higher. At 16,000 MT capacity I'm looking at $384 million in revenue instead. Again though, brining this back to reality, AREC's stake would come in at $72.9 million instead. Still highly attractive in my opinion, putting it at a FWD P/S of 3.2x right now. For a high growth company like this with potential to realize even more in revenue depending on the product mix, I think a P/S of 4x is realistic here. That is a 25% upside in the short-term (12 - 18 months). This would beat out almost all market indices and ETFs, precisely what I'm looking for right now.

Even with a conservative estimate that AREC will only scale up to 25% of capacity by 2026 year end, at roughly 2x sales it still suggests that AREC might be a bargain in the market right now.

I think the bull case looks even stronger when you now also consider that AREC will be processing Nd/Pr as well, which trades at $218 as of today (April 8, 2026). I’m not including that though because neither I nor any analyst I think knows the exact product mix that will be once production comes online. $24 per kilo process is the floor, so it could only really go higher from there in theory. Unless the price of lithium falls of course. But with global consumption set to rise by 13.5% this year, I think the case is weak that prices would decline in the short-term, or the medium-term for that matter too.

The issue I can see here is that because the actual yield that ReElement would have isn't public, it makes any revenue projections come under pressure. But, because lithium trades at a much lower price than any of the other rare earth minerals like Neodymium or Scandium. My point is that the likelihood is high that any model with a blend of lithium and RRE will be a lot higher in terms of net selling price than the $24/kg I've used.

Looking Beyond 16,000 MT Capacity

The commonly mentioned figure of 16,000 MT capacity is not the end goal for AREC. As the Marion facility includes 400,000-square feet, AREC has a lot of potential to increase production lines. Because it uses chromatography columns, scaling becomes both faster and requires less space too.

with only around 160,000 square feet currently in use

That is the quote I want to use to give you an idea of the future capacity expansion potential. If 16,000 MT equals 160,000 then that leaves 240,000 square-feet of unused space still. If the ratio of space and capacity stays the same, AREC would have 40,000 MT in processing capacity per year. I think it’s a bit crude to just straight up use that. But it should certainly leave room for at least 30,000 MT per year. This is a near 100% increase from what is already an ambitious target set for Q3 this year. As a small note though, ReElement has a lot of potential to expand production, but obviously scaling won't be linear due to shared infrastructure.

But I think importantly it leaves a clear roadmap for where the company is heading right now. For the sake of it, at “full” utilization of the space, i.e 30,000 MT the revenues given today's lithium price would be $720 million, or $136.8 million for AREC. It’s not out of the realm of possibility that AREC reaches that by the end of this decade if they keep up their current pace.

Risks

The main risk with this thesis is that AREC once again falls back to its habits about delays to projects. Now we have a deadline of Q3 2026 for production to start. Investors expect to see revenues in that quarter and a drastic ramp up in Q4 2026 as well to prove that the theory holds true. If utilization rates fall short of estimates, meaning AREC is barely able to break 10% of total capacity I think the market will punish the stock price.

Given the exposure it has to commodity prices as well there is an added risk level there too. Much like an upstream or downstream company, AREC will be exposed to volatility. Between 2022 and 2024 the lithium market experienced massive oversupply as production increased in China but also Australia. It has since stabilized and rallied even, but it doesn't mean that another wave of oversupply could hit.

I see this risk mainly coming from EV affordability declining which would reduce end-market demand and in turn demand for lithium batteries. So far EV prices continue to fall. Higher interest rates would also pressure demand. But on the contrary to this, because of the U.S. military taking steps to securing a domestic supply chain of rare earth minerals, this demand could help offset any weakness seen in the EV market.

The Full Picture

AREC has improved in my eyes from what was a Hold rating to a now Buy. I think the case is pretty clear. AREC is rapidly increasing it’s capacity and at even conservative utilization estimates the potential revenue generation looks incredibly attractive, but importantly also, it’s highly scalable. The partnership with MMC ensures that it has the necessary feedstock, alleviating another risk. But, given the past history of project delays along with the announcement the 2025 10-K would come later, I see the stock trading at a depressed valuation until management can regain the market’s trust and surpass estimates. On the contrary, I get to buy a solid company at a strong discount relative to its total potential near-term revenues. I’ll gladly take this risk. Upgrading my rating to a Buy.

data.title: G10 Currency Consolidation Looks Constructive, But The Weekend Poses Risks
data.description:
The dollar is mixed against the G10 currencies today, ahead of the March US CPI report. The dollar bloc and the Japanese yen are struggling. However, the tone is mostly consolidative. Equities were higher in the Asia Pacific region, with a few exceptions, and in Europe. Bond yields are firmer. Both WTI and Brent crude oil are trading with a slightly firmer bias but well within this week’s ranges.

US and Iranian officials will meet in Pakistan tomorrow. The ceasefire apparently seen some violations, and the Strait of Hormuz is not fully open as it was before the war. Yet, market participants seem hopeful. At the same time, reports suggest Ukraine and Russia may be near a tentative agreement to stop hostilities. Meanwhile, reports suggest Federal Reserve Chair Powell and Treasury Secretary Bessent met with the large banks to discuss new risks generated by AI. Hungary goes to the polls this weekend, and Prime Minister Orban is lagging in the polls. He is liked by the Trump administration (though its support may have cost him) and Russia and China. Still, the Hungarian forint is the second strongest emerging market currency this week (~3.6%), lagging behind the Russian ruble’s 3.7% appreciation.

Prices

G10

• The euro traded in the upper end of Wednesday’s trading range yesterday and again stalled in front of $1.1725. It is consolidating mostly between $1.1680 and slightly above $1.1715 so far today. The price action looks constructive. The next technical target is $1.1745-50. It has a four-day advancing streak coming into today, which matches the longest of the year.

• The dollar traded firmly against the yen yesterday but could not resurface above JPY159.30. It has edged slightly higher today but is holding below JPY159.40. $780 mln of options at JPY159.50 expire today. The greenback settled slightly above JPY159.65 last week. If the dollar does not recover and settle above it today, it will be the first back-to-back losing weeks for the dollar since the end of January.

• Sterling consolidated in the upper end of Wednesday’s range yesterday. It hardly spent any time below Wednesday’s $1.3395 settlement. Sterling has held above $1.3400 today, where options for GBP355 mln expire today. On the other hand, it is holding below yesterday’s high (~$1.3460). Wednesday’s high was almost $1.3485, its best level since the war began.

• The Canadian dollar strengthened to its best level in a little more than two weeks yesterday. The greenback was sold to almost CAD1.3800. It frayed the 20- and 200-day moving averages (~CAD1.3815-20) on an intraday basis but settled above them. The US dollar is trading firmly and reached almost CAD1.3845 today. Options for nearly $600 mln at CAD1.3850 expire today.

• The Australian dollar reached a three-week high yesterday, a little shy of $.7100. At the end of March, it fell below $0.6840. The five-day moving average crossed above the 20-day moving average yesterday for the first time since mid-March. It is trading with a slightly softer bias today and is consolidating between about $0.7055 and $0.7085. A band of nearby resistance extends toward $0.7125.

EM

• The Mexican peso extended its recovery and has recovered the lion’s share of last month’s losses. The US dollar finished February near MXN17.2270. Yesterday’s losses took the greenback a little through MXN17.3250. It is in roughly a MXN17.3450-MXN17.4045 range today. The dollar peaked on March 31 near MXN18.1650. It posted a key downside reversal that day and has not looked back.

• The dollar drew closer to the next important support area against the offshore yuan near CNH6.80 yesterday. It reached CNH6.8200 on Wednesday, a three-year low and consolidated above it yesterday. Since Wednesday’s low, the greenback has not been above CNH6.8420. Today, it has not been above CNH6.8345. The PBOC set the dollar’s reference rate at CNY6.8654 (CNY6.8649 yesterday, a three-year low). The low fix in 2023 was around CNY6.7130.

• It appears that the Indian currency controls introduced recently have run their course. The US dollar briefly traded to a marginal new low since March 18 today (~INR92.4115) but recovered to almost INR92.7665. Still, the rupee managed to hold on to a 0.40% increase for the week.

Other Markets

• Asia Pacific and European equities have traded firmer today. Most of the large bourses in the Asia Pacific region rose by at least 1% today, though Hong Kong’s Hang Seng was an exception (+0.55%) as were Australia (-0.15%) and New Zealand (-0.70%). Europe’s Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is up around 0.60% in late morning turnover. If the gains are maintained, it would be the second consecutive week that it rose more than 3%. US index futures are hovering around little-changed levels. The S&P 500 is up about 3.6% this week, and the Nasdaq is up about 4.3%.

• Benchmark 10-year yields have jumped today. Australia's and Japan’s rates rose a little more than five basis points. European yields are 4-8 bp higher. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is up almost two basis points to approach 4.30%. It is off a little more than three basis points on the week.

• Gold is trading slightly heavier today but within yesterday’s range (~$4699-$4801). Silver is consolidating between about $74.85 and $76.20. Both metals are up for the third consecutive week.

• May WTI is firm but quiet. It is near $99 in late European morning activity. It reached $102.70 yesterday and is holding below $100.50 today. The month’s low was recorded on Wednesday, near $91. Today’s low is near $97.60.

Data

• The reaction to the expected surge in the March US CPI will be tempered by the ceasefire. The median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey is for a 1% rise in CPI last month, which would lift the year-over-year pace to 3.4% from 2.4%. The core is seen rising by 0.3% for a 2.7% year-over-year increase. If the ceasefire holds and leads to an end to the conflict, there still may be some residual upward pressure on CPI in April. The ceasefire comes too late to lower inflation expectations in the preliminary April University of Michigan survey. The preliminary durable goods orders (-1.4% headline and +0.6% excluding defense and aircraft orders) removes some interest from today’s factory goods orders and revision to the durable goods report. Lastly, the March federal budget deficit is due, and the median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey is for a shortfall of about $152.5 bln. This compares to $160.5 bln deficit in March 2025 and $236.6 bln deficit in March 2024.

• After losing almost 84k jobs in February (-108.4k full-time positions were lost and part-time jobs rose by 24.5k), Canada is expected to report a muted recovery with a gain of about 15k jobs overall. The participation rate may have ticked up to 65.0% from 64.9%, and this will likely be translated into a rise in the unemployment rate to 6.8% from 6.7%.

• Mexico reports February industrial production figures today. After falling 1.1% in January, it is expected to have recovered by about 0.6%. Still, the year-over-year pace of both industrial output and manufacturing production is still falling on a year-over-year basis (-1.0% and -1.8%, respectively), which appears to be a greater concern to the central bank than inflation, which we learned yesterday remained above the 2-4% target range last month.

• Japan reported a 0.8% jump in March producer prices. The February series was revised to 0.1% from -0.1%. Given the base effect, this increased the year-over-year rate more modestly to 2.6% from a revised 2.1% (initially 2.0%), which is the highest since last November. Separately, Japan reported a 28.1% year-over-year jump in machine tool orders (24.2% in February). Foreign orders surged 40.4%, while domestic orders rose 2.5%. The market has wavered a bit, but we continue to suspect that there is a better chance that the Bank of Japan lifts rates later this month than it standing pat.

• China reported PPI rose 0.5% in March, its first year-over-year increase since September 2022. Deflationary forces have been slackening since the middle of last year, when PPI fell 3.6% year-over-year. Since then, with one exception, deflation in producer prices has moderated. The CPI is 1.0% higher than a year ago (1.3% in February). Previously, many observers had attributed the weakness in CPI to weak demand. We have been more skeptical. Consumption has risen in China, but investment has increased quicker, and food prices, for which demand is more inelastic, seem to be a key factor. Consider that the slippage in March seemed to reflect the moderation in food price increases (0.3%) after 1.7% in February. Also, housing prices fell for the fourth consecutive month. Core CPI finished 2025 at 1.2% and rose to 1.8% in February. It moderated to 1.1% in March.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Hamilton Lane: Dip In Price, Not In Value
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

There have been a few well-known trends during 2026.

At the beginning of the year, growth investments sold off over fear that the AI trade had gotten ahead of itself. We've also seen private credit investments heavily hit and beaten down, with private credit managers selling off over fear of credit cockroaches, which was a fear popularized by the CEO of JPMorgan (JPM). This fear spread to private equity, in general, that the opportunities and deals they were making were likely overly risky for the value that they were returning. Now we've shifted to commodities becoming something people are concerned about, both originally with Venezuela dropping oil prices and now Iran hiking them.

I love, as a data-driven, fundamentals-focused investor, to look beyond the news cycle and the noise floating around the market at the underlying fundamentals of a company. If the short-term share price moves rapidly, but the fundamentals of the company remain rock solid, I can recognize this as a sentimental shift, not a value shift. This can cause the companies' share price to become extremely undervalued versus what the company is truly worth at the fundamental level.

Today, I want to take a look at a company that I highlighted at the beginning of the year before the wheels came off much of the market, and I want to look at it again and see if there has been a cataclysmic value change at the fundamental level or whether this is all sentimental noise blowing around so we can take a step to move beyond the chaos of day-to-day life and find clarity in the truth.

Let's dive in!

The Democratization Of Private Markets

Operating across almost two dozen offices in North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia, Hamilton Lane Incorporated (HLNE) provides clients with services, such as private equity/credit, venture capital, infrastructure, and real estate. As of December 31st, 2025, the company had $1.02 trillion in assets under management ($871.5 billion)/advisement ($146.1 billion).

HLNE's Q3 2026 earnings report demonstrated its fundamentals are holding up well. The company's total revenue climbed 18% higher year-over-year to $198.6 million in the quarter, beating the analyst consensus by $3 million.

This was due to a combination of a 6.4% growth in the combined AUM/A and an ongoing shift to specialized funds and the Evergreen platform. That led management and advisory fees to rise by 21.3% over the year-ago period to $153.2 million during the quarter. The company's incentive fees also grew by 10.2% year-over-year to $45.4 million for the quarter.

HLNE's non-GAAP EPS rose 24% higher over the year-ago period to $1.55 in the fiscal third quarter. This surpassed the analyst consensus during the quarter by $0.22. The biggest contributor to this bottom-line growth was the 70% year-over-year spike in Evergreen AUM to $16 billion for the quarter. Since these specialized funds have much higher fees than traditional customized separate accounts, the non-GAAP net profit margin expanded by about 210 basis points to 42.5% in the quarter.

In the years to come, HLNE looks like it has plenty of room to grow further. This is because the company's own 2026 Private Global Wealth Survey found 97% of private wealth professionals surveyed allocated between 1% and 20% of their book of business to private markets. And the vast majority of those surveyed (86%) planned to increase their private market investments this year, citing portfolio optimization.

That's why we think the F.A.S.T. Graphs analyst consensus of 15.3% annual non-GAAP EPS growth through FY 2028, off a FY 2025 base of $5.04, is achievable.

HLNE is also financially stable. The company's interest coverage ratio in the first three quarters of FY 2026 stood at just shy of 60x.

HLNE's 2.2% dividend yield is nearly double the broader market, too. Better yet, the company's non-GAAP EPS payout ratio is likely to be in the high-30% range for FY 2026. This is why we're confident that HLNE can lengthen its eight-year dividend growth streak with more 10%+ payout boosts (for a Chowder number just above 12).

From the current $97 share price (April 9th), the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 15.9. For more perspective, that's well below the eight-year average P/E ratio of 25.3, per F.A.S.T. Graphs.

In the years ahead, we believe that a rational fair value P/E ratio is 23. HLNE's annual non-GAAP EPS growth consensus of 15.3% is moderately below the eight-year average CAGR of 23.9%. But when adjusting for its larger size and scale, this suggests that HLNE's growth profile is mostly intact.

That's why we think that a fair value multiple of one standard deviation below the eight-year average is attainable. This produces a fair value per share estimate of $145, which is a 31% discount to fair value from current levels.

If HLNE returns to fair value and matches growth forecasts, it could post a 46% total return (48% annualized) by the end of March 2027.

Key risks to HLNE include the semi-liquidity structure of its Evergreen funds and talent retention.

As Kody indicated in a recent article on Brookfield Asset Management (BAM), the current sentiment in private markets is a risk to watch. A few weeks ago, BlackRock (BLK) limited withdrawals on a flagship $26 billion private credit fund (HLEND) after redemption requests were 9.3%, nearly doubling the 5% quarterly limit.

If HLNE is forced to follow suit by halting redemptions or if it has to sell assets at a discount, this would damage its brand. That could have lasting implications on fundraising capacity in the future.

Another risk to HLNE is that its continued success is largely dependent on human capital. This requires recruiting and retaining the brightest minds in the business. A failure to do so would likely undermine its ability to deliver satisfactory results to clients. That could lead to a client exodus, which would pressure HLNE's ability to reach growth targets.

Conclusion

There's no doubt in my mind that HLNE was overvalued as we moved into the beginning of the year. I highlighted it on January 2nd, saying that it was finally back at an attractive buy price once more. However, the market decided to double down on the attractiveness of its buy price by selling it off further due to the fears of sentiment blowing around. When we look at the fundamental health of the company, the data is clear. The company remains extremely well-positioned and is generating earnings growth that is highly attractive while rewarding shareholders. Unlike some companies that routinely buy back their shares, which helps provide a bottom to how far they will fall. HLNE at this point hasn't announced any sort of buyback program. This means that until investor sentiment turns around, HLNE might continue to sell off. I find them extremely attractive here as a company. However, there is the potential that it will fall further before it climbs, and if you are a patient investor who does not want to endure that continued decline, I would recommend that you continue to stand by and watch until the bottom has been found. If you're an investor who finds this price attractive compared to its historic value, like I do, it's an excellent opportunity to "buy" it.

Part of building a financial fortress around your retirement is recognizing the value of each block and brick you use to build it. HLNE is still an extremely high-quality company, but now the price per brick is even lower than it was before. This doesn't mean that the company's value has declined, only its short-term trading price, and in the long term, you can enjoy strong returns from it.

Understanding the difference between the value of a company and the price that you pay can help you step beyond the chaos of day-to-day market noise and enter into the clarity that royalty live in. The clarity of being a Dividend King.

data.title: Micron: Crushing Cyclical Fears (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Upgrading Micron Back To "Buy"

I became cautious on Micron Technology (MU) stock when I saw extraordinary greed coming from retail investors back in October last year, and I reiterated that view in January 2026. In my most recent update in particular, I feared that big institutional capital was about to shake out those who bought close to the local top. Time passed and the correction in MU's price action did take place, but I was unable to upgrade MU back to "Buy" in a timely fashion when the sell-off had gone too far 1-2 weeks ago, and MU has managed to gain back over 34% from its March 2026 lows.

I should say that the price action isn't the reason for my today's upgrade. I'm genuinely amused by the number of negative revisions on both the top and bottom lines that have started to appear lately, despite strongly bullish news coming in at the very same time. I understand the "cyclical top" fears of some investors and speculators, but the current discrepancy between what's happening in the core end markets in terms of demand, supply, and pricing, and what's being reflected in MU's forward consensus estimates, is exactly why I believe Micron is set to beat next quarter and move higher. It's not "just the beginning" of this rally, but a continuation that should help MU close the undervaluation gap in the coming weeks, in my view.

Updating My Reasoning On Micron

First off, as I usually do, let me quickly take a look at Micron's latest financials. The company reported $23.86 billion in its fiscal Q2 2026, which was up 196% YoY, beating the consensus estimate by almost 21% - the greatest beat from MU for the past 16 quarters at least, according to Seeking Alpha:

Gross margins expanded by 1800 bps on a QoQ basis, reaching a historical high of 74.9% and enabling massive growth at the bottom-line level - adjusted EPS came in at $12.20 at a time when the consensus was waiting for $9.16 (a beat of over 33%, the highest over the past 12 quarters).

One might have thought that MU should have risen significantly on this earnings report, but it didn't move. Moreover, in the few trading sessions following the Q2 release, Micron managed to lose almost 1/3 of its market cap. Why? First, the management raised its CAPEX plans for 2026 to over $25 billion, with the construction-related CAPEX amounting to likely over $10 billion in fiscal 2027. Bears have become cautious, recalling what CAPEX expansion means when memory chip makers' stocks rise by hundreds of percentage points in a short period of time - when demand strength subsides, the newly built capacity becomes idle and excess supply leads to pricing drops, hence lower revenues and earnings. Second, the management guided for 81% in GP margin for Q3, and some might have considered it the ultimate peak for Micron, or the top of the cycle. Third, after the Q2 print, we've seen that Google (GOOG) released "TurboQuant", which is basically a compression algorithm that reduces the memory overhead needed to run LLMs in a big way. The panic sell-off on the memory chip market intensified on that news.

But I disagree with the market's initial reaction - now we're seeing that MU, along with its peers, has been healing, and it looks like the recovery is here to stay for a few reasons.

Let me gently disagree with the above-mentioned fears first and explain my thought process. On CAPEX - I don't think it's a problem for Micron. On the contrary, it's a very smart choice if we consider the current demand side as strengthening in a structural, not cyclical, way. The demand is rising much more rapidly than what we're used to before, but the supply side hasn't expanded by relatively the same amount. Micron, as the biggest US representative, will likely start to fill the demand/supply gap in the next few years, and it's going to lead to lower pricing, I agree, but the top line is very likely to be boosted by the volumes rise. The CAPEX updates we've heard are a critical and hard-to-avoid reality for the whole tech industry, and I'd actually be more bullish if Micron guided for a higher amount, because the return on investment in the current environment should logically be far superior to before.

In Micron's case, what I like the most is the execution that lays out ground for future expansion in a high-quality (profitable) way. The management promised earlier that they'll catch up in the HBM race, matching their market share there to their overall DRAM share by 2025. They achieved it in fiscal Q3 2025 by passing by earlier iterations and going straight to HBM3E, thus securing crucial design-ins for Nvidia's (NVDA) H200 and B200 GPUs. Another thing the management was targeting initially was the high-margin data center storage end market. For 4 years (through 2025), Micron has been expanding its enterprise SSD market share, and today, the high-margin enterprise SSDs make up roughly 50% of the company's NAND revenue, which is de-risking Micron from the commoditized consumer SSD market to at least some extent. The execution at Micron looks top-notch to me, and I believe it's about to last further.

At the recent NVDA GTC conference, Micron announced it has entered high-volume production of its HBM4 36GB 12-High memory stacks built specifically for NVDA's new architecture, the Vera Rubin AI platform. They're going to be the first memory supplier to bring HBM4, the PCIe Gen6 data center SSD, and the SOCAMM2 module to volume shipment simultaneously for this new system, and if we consider the current pricing stability for Micron's chips, I suppose it's going to contribute a lot to the top line in 2027-2028, effectively extending the cycle.

On the new algorithm from Google, I agree with plenty of other analysts who view it as an accelerator of demand and usage for memory chips - the 'Jevons Paradox' historically appears in cases where new tech makes computing cheaper and more efficient. So, it doesn't mean demand for MU's offerings will fall off a cliff in the coming quarters - the cause-and-effect relationships bears had in mind seem to be off, in my opinion.

Now, let me jump right to the current discrepancy between what's happening on the memory chips market and the earnings revisions that took place over the past month or so.

According to TrendForce, conventional DRAM contract prices are expected to rise 58% to 63% quarter-over-quarter in Q2 2026 after the massive increase of 90-95% in Q1. This news came out on April 1st, 2026. Yesterday, on April 9th, TrendForce wrote that Transcend, a Taiwanese module maker, reportedly sees a 40–50% DDR5 price surge in Q2 this year, only solidifying the previous price hikes outlook.

Liberty Times, citing Shu, notes that supply from major vendors such as Samsung and Micron remains constrained, with fulfillment rates still at very low levels. He added that while Transcend has signed long-term agreements (LTAs) with suppliers, full supply commitments cannot be guaranteed under current extreme tightness.

Source: TrendForce, April 9, 2026

Knowing that Micron's entire HBM supply is completely sold out through the calendar year 2026 (with substantial portions of 2027 capacity already committed), and that margins are set to rise to ~81% in Q3, I see limited risks to what's coming after Q3 in terms of both sales and margins. For some reasons unknown to me, we started to see negative revisions to both the top and bottom lines over the past month:

I view it as a temporary reaction to the bearish headlines that I disagreed with above. It looks like the analysts have only made it easier for Micron to beat its upcoming earnings. The firm reports in June, so it's not my final earnings preview conclusion, but it looks to me that amid the currently durable contract pricing dynamics, Micron remains discounted for the wrong reason.

Assuming that Micron beats the fiscal FY2027 EPS by only 5%, the implied price-to-earnings multiple arrives at ~4.1x:

$421.51 / ($97.94 * 1.05) = ~4.098x

Source: Oakoff's calculations

Even if the market's fears don't subside en masse, and MU keeps trading at only 5-6x earnings next year, there will be an upside potential of over 34% if my math is correct. This conclusion points to a slightly higher-than-consensus PT, but no matter how we perceive the exit P/E, I think in most cases Micron would look too cheap to ignore, even at the risk of some chip pricing weakness ahead - the firm has locked in premium prices to some extent, de-risking its revenues anyway.

Risks

There's still a cyclical risk, which is probably the biggest bears' argument right away. As I wrote in my previous Hold-rated article on Micron, the management might know it in advance, which explains the insider selling transactions:

I think Micron might register a few new ATHs from here, but it'll likely face heavy profit-taking once the above trend becomes more obvious to the market participants. I can only guess here, but what if that's the reason for the insiders' selling? They understand these cycles best, and that may be why they are quietly heading for the exits, while ordinary investors risk getting trapped by buying into a stock trading at ATHs just as the company commits to decades of heavy capital outflows.

Source: Oakoff's update on MU, January 2026

Indeed, I might be underestimating the cycles this time, calling them different, whereas they might turn out to be similar to those we've seen before. If pricing eases and Micron fails to compensate with larger volumes, we'll see no multiple expansion and earnings growth at the same time - in that scenario, the odds of seeing lower earnings rise dramatically.

Your Takeaway

Despite the cyclicality risks, I like MU's momentum strength - both in operational and stock price action terms. I think the management will keep executing on its CAPEX plans well, and the new offerings and collaborations with tech giants like NVDA will bring more to the top line at relatively high margins. The stock looks undervalued when we add a reasonable premium on the forward earnings consensus - I see an upside of over 34% today. Based on all this, I decided to upgrade my previously neutral stance on Micron to "Buy" today.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: ULTY: Broken Then, Better Now, Misread Always
data.description:
It is unfortunate to see the markets show a lot of interest in the YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (ULTY), when there were apparent structural flaws for most of 2025. But, as the flaws seemed to have been ironed out better now, interest in ULTY has been waning considerably. I see that as a mistimed product understanding. What should have been an opportunistic Buy around the tariff sell-off lows last year, and a more cautious approach around August-September turned out to be otherwise. Interest kept picking up till reverse stock splits potentially raised concerns. Coincidentally, it was around December YieldMax fixed some vital flaws in the portfolio and strategy, making it no worse than several other strong covered call income ETFs.

Markets seem to be reacting to developments and most recent performances so far when it comes to ULTY, rather than truly understanding its positioning, strategy changes and projecting ahead, rather than simply extrapolating recent performance. I have been able to time the calls on ULTY reasonably well because I have been trying to understand what has been going on behind the high yield screen.

This analysis is a follow-up to the Buy rating issued in December on structural improvements. At the time, we did not have empirical data to prove expectations ahead. We have more than 3 months of data now, and that includes a period of high volatility, first through a phase where markets started correcting long duration growth multiples, and then geopolitical tensions and stagflation worries. The data through this testing period shows ULTY has indeed held up far better than even some more popular index-level option writing strategies. I expect more of that to continue. So, ULTY should rank quite high up now for income investors, especially those looking for high yields and quick de-risking from the markets.

Pros and Cons of High Yields

One of the major concerns I had around ULTY from the start is its aggressively high yield that almost ensures NAV erosion. That has not changed. A plot of the weekly payout as a % of prevalent prices shows that payouts are still aggressive - 1.5% on average of the share price at any given point of time. The structural changes have happened on the strategy and portfolio, not the yield. They continue to be aggressive, and in the absence of commensurate income from the option layer to fund these payouts, investors receive a significant chunk of payouts in the form of ROC, that is invested capital and appreciation if any (plus some option income, if any).

This is something that cannot be argued against - NAV destruction is often permanent. It decreases the check size of future payouts and also keeps less on the table to grow or rebound (if payouts coincided with a fall in the underlying holdings). Investors for whom NAV erosion matters, ULTY is still an avoidable vehicle. But investors who are looking for an enhanced cash flow tool, ULTY could serve as a mechanism whereby investments are de-risked quickly (convert equity exposure into cash flow). For such investors, the ULTY strategy harvests volatility while payouts are made - cashflow that could be used to fund near term liabilities or withdrawal goals. This is still an area of caution - the high yields - but I am just being mindful of use cases, where investors with the right set of expectations could find value in the high yields. For investors who care about long term compounding alongside payouts, reinvestments can be a route to still use ULTY, but that is not the most friendly route (compared to a low yield vehicle) due to tax tracking complexities.

Summary of Fixes and Last 3 Months

The more important concern about ULTY, and something I believe is better addressed now, is the structural drag in total returns. More important, because higher yields are not money lost, while worse total returns is. ULTY has moved on from a high volatile and narrative driven portfolio to a more stable, cash-flow-backed portfolio, while retaining a growth flavor. The option layer has also changed from a rigid and aggressive almost fully covered call writing layer to a more flexible, potentially partially covered and hedged option positioning. The round trip losses seen earlier where upsides were capped, and downsides were almost fully open (minus the option premium), is now structurally addressed. This is why I had upgraded ULTY to a Buy in December, at a time when the sentiments were negative around reverse splits.

Now, we have empirical data showing how the renewed strategy has not only held up compared to a less growth-focused and broader portfolio like SPY, but often outperformed significantly. And all this through a market regime when growth themes were under pressure.

Interestingly, inflows and interest in ULTY has not picked up after the changes. The below chart shows how the AUM has broadly moved in sync with prices over the past 3 months post changes. For context, you can see how ULTY's AUM swelled during the initial part of the rebound from the April 2025 lows, at the wrong time (when structural flaws existed as seen from the longer trend of ULTY vs SPY total returns chart). The ULTY total returns rode a high risk high growth portfolio from April 2025 to somewhere around September and crashed heavily as the growth rally stalled. Many investors seem to have dumped ULTY at the wrong time and using the wrong signal of reverse stock splits. The time to dump ULTY was around soon after the rebound rally in Q3 2025 (tactical growth play). And the time to reinvest was in December. And since then, the total return charts do not show great outperformance - we have a more stable portfolio today that should not deviate from the SPY by much, especially on the downside. You can read more about the portfolio changes that support the more stable underlying portfolio here. This is what I wrote in December -

the current portfolio today is already showing a greater proportion of real assets (such as bitcoin, Treasury bonds, and gold) and cash-flow-anchored stocks. Jabil Inc. (JBL), Quanta Services, Inc. (PWR), HCA Healthcare, Inc. (HCA), and Corning Incorporated (GLW) are stocks that are strongly correlated to CapEx cycles, services, and recurring demand, not narratives. Then we now have more mature and margin-rich semis like Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), Lam Research Corporation (LRCX), Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI), and Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT). There are real asset-tied stocks in metals, materials, and inflation hedges like Alcoa Corporation (AA) and Southern Copper Corporation (SCCO) (and a miner ETF). The rest of the portfolio is still growth-tilted, but cash-backed rather than just concept-backed, with names like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), etc.

Further Changes and Current Positioning

In December, I was awaiting more data around how the option strategy is implemented and how active they remain. The current holdings (downloaded from the ETF website) indicates that there is no evidence of a less aggressive covered call writing. For some of the top holdings, like Amazon, Jabil and Google, we still see fully covered call writing positioning (Amazon's 1600 calls written is equivalent to the underlying shares applying a contract size of 100, for example). This needs to be observed further to see whether this kind of aggressive call writing coverage remains in bull market conditions (because the last 3 months have not been that) or not.

However, one thing has definitely improved, and drastically so. And that is net long put positions that almost completely reduces downsides. Almost because unlike in Amazon, downsides open up beyond a lower strike for Google and Jabil (put spreads). But the upside capping drawback is handled better, by reducing the downside asymmetry to a large extent. The setup of partial versus full drawdown mitigation is an active call seemingly depending on the management's view of individual stocks. In short, I was expecting less option coverage in terms of call writing. That is not seen. That has been compensated by a stronger long put hedge layer.

Given the upside cap and a better drawdown protection, the net income is likely to be lower from options alone (because some of the net gains from sold options are used to fund long put positions). However, it drastically reduces portfolio volatility in total return terms.

That is more important because some of the holdings are still high growth and high volatility (although an improvement from the pre-December era). With a more limited drawdown setup now, ULTY effectively harnesses the higher premiums of high growth stocks, and does not stay exposed to its higher risks to the same extent.

The portfolio components have also seen a lot of change since December and it will be interesting to see whether ULTY keeps this underlying layer as significantly as well across regimes. The changes toward a lower volatile portfolio matter less given how there are puts in place to reduce risks. However, given that in some cases ULTY uses put spreads, they should be analyzed to see how risky the portfolio looks today.

Since December, the underlying portfolio seems to have moved to a more cyclical and infrastructure driven mix. Treasury exposure and defensives like HCA, Eli Lilly and Intuit have been removed, while metals exposure has broadened through additions like SILJ along with retained positions in GDX and Southern Copper (I say broadened because GLD has been removed). CapEx and infrastructure names such as Ciena, Lumentum and Comfort Systems have also been added, strengthening the industrial and optical cycle exposure. Crypto exposure has shifted from IBIT to MicroStrategy, increasing beta to crypto from December. Growth exposure remains, but is now concentrated in higher-quality names like NVIDIA rather than earlier defensive growth holdings. Overall, there is some rejig toward slightly higher beta and higher growth themes from defensive in December. It is still looking largely a less volatile and risky portfolio than the pre-December setup. The put hedges may have emboldened the return to growth bets - although it's still well balanced.

Expect More of the Same

I find the ULTY portfolio a decent balance of growth, high beta and commodity/cyclical plays. And the long put hedge changes the risk-return profile meaningfully. ULTY is now attempting to harness volatility while trying to keep drawdowns better managed than the pre-December era.

Importantly, this renewed avatar has seen ULTY emerge as a challenger to several other popular and strong index covered call alternatives. Below is a chart of total returns against SPYI, one of my favorite income plays using covered calls.

I expect more of the same to continue and reiterate a Buy rating for ULTY. I will keep an eye on how actively the underlying portfolio is churned, because that could mean another source of active selection alpha. I will also keep a watch on whether ULTY changes the option strategy depending on stocks and market conditions (for example lighter call writing or a totally hedged put layer across holdings). But the current setup is revealing enough to re-enter, especially those who quit ULTY for flaws that are not applicable today.

data.title: VXUS: Buying International Equity Still Has Upside - Strong Buy
data.description:
The Vanguard Total International Stock ETF (VXUS) seeks to track the performance of a benchmark index that measures the investment return of stocks issued by companies located in developed and emerging markets, excluding the United States.

I am initiating VXUS on Seeking Alpha as a strong buy, as this ETF offers exposure to grow strongly versus a falling dollar, a relative value discount versus U.S. equity, and ex-US earnings growth that is converging with the S&P 500 on consensus estimates.

On a 12-month forward basis, the MSCI ACWI ex-USD Index trades at approx. 14.6x earnings versus 22.8x for the S&P 500. This spread is the widest it has been since the GFC. Additionally, the price to cash earnings ratio of MSCI US is 2.2x the ACWI ex-US figure, which is approx. a 50% premium above the median valuation over a long period of time.

The U.S. Dollar

The general estimate from Cambridge Associates shows that the U.S. Dollar is approx. 32% higher than its real value based on equity weight. I believe that after some recent strengthening of the dollar due to the recent geopolitical conflict, I believe that the dollar will resume its weakening trend throughout this year as situations normalize.

Fidelity's research further shows that the DXY was down by approx. 9% for 2025, within December 2025, and the current tailwind had driven ex-U.S. equities to approx. 30% USD returns in 2025, while the S&P 500 returned 17.9%. Due to the lack of FX hedging in VXUS, all basis points of dollar weakness can flow directly to U.S. base holders as a gain due to foreign currency fluctuations. This is the reason I favor VXUS over a nonhedged alternative, as the outlook on the dollar, in my opinion, will fuel additional growth due to currency fluctuations on top of ex-U.S. equities growth.

Earnings

In recent years, the bear case against international equity involved ex-U.S. companies being cheaper, as they deserved to be cheaper. U.S. EPS growth had consistently outrun ex-U.S. EPS growth. This assumption is now weakening, as Schwab's 2026 international outlook shows that ex-U.S. earnings are expected to grow into double-digit rates after struggling in a flat range within 2025, with EUR and JPY financials industries leading the pack. Additionally, AllianceBernstein notes in their equity outlook that emerging markets, as well as developed markets, outpaced the S&P500 in 2025, even when denominating to local currency. This assists in the buy thesis for VXUS, as it is not just a FX thesis, but also an equities growth story as well. Between JPY corporate governance reform, Euro bank net interest margin expansion, and Emerging Markets technology buildouts, this results in a appealing blend of international developed and emerging market growth that will compliment VXUS nicely, on top of the way FX dynamics will affect international equity.

Concentration

The S&P500 has approx. 40% of the index's weight within the top 10 companies at the end of 2025. VXUS is a nice way to diversify from this exposure, as it holds more than 8,000 companies with no single company over 1% of the fund's holdings. For someone already exposed to the cap-weighted S&P, or even someone exposed to the equal-weight variant of the S&P, VXUS is an amazing single name diversifier against mega-caps, or the overall S&P, underperforming.

Inside of VXUS

VXUS tracks the FTSE Global All Cap ex US Index, which as of October 2024 contained approximately 8,408 stocks across 47 markets.
